overview.body: Chapter 10 introduces the biggest shift in thinking you will encounter in this course. Until now, your programs have been collections of functions and variables. Classes change that. They let you bundle data and the functions that work on that data into a single unit called an object. Programs 10-9 and 10-10 build a BankAccount class with a special __str__ method. Programs 10-12 and 10-13 build a CellPhone class that demonstrates encapsulation through private attributes and getter/setter methods. Type each one carefully. The indentation and double-underscore naming conventions are not optional.
objectives: Define a class with private attributes using the double-underscore naming convention
Write an __init__ constructor method that initializes object attributes
Write getter and setter methods to access and modify private attributes
Implement the __str__ special method for custom string representation
Create object instances and call methods using dot notation
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 10 covers class definition (Section 10.2), constructors and instance methods (Section 10.2), encapsulation with private attributes (Section 10.3), and the __str__ special method (Section 10.3). Programs 10-9, 10-10, 10-12, and 10-13 demonstrate these techniques.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis, Chapter 10
programRequirements: All four programs are implemented and run without errors
Programs 10-9 and 10-12 define the class only and contain no main() function
Programs 10-10 and 10-13 import their respective class module and test it
Each program is in its own clearly commented section
File has a top-of-file documentation header: Name, abc123, Section, Description
overview.filenameNote: Submit bookex-ch10.py with all four programs combined into one file.

rows.task: Test BankAccount objects — import, create, call methods, print object
CellPhone class — private attributes, getters, setters
Test CellPhone objects — import, create, call getters

content.body:
Program 10-9 — BankAccount Class with __str__
Define a BankAccount class that manages a balance. This file contains only the class definition, with no main() function and no input() calls.

items: Define class BankAccount:. All the methods below go inside this class, indented one level.
Write __init__(self, bal): store the balance in a private attribute self.__balance = bal.
Write deposit(self, amount): add the amount to self.__balance using +=.
Write withdraw(self, amount): check if self.__balance >= amount. If so, subtract it. If not, print an insufficient funds error message.
Write get_balance(self): return self.__balance.
Write __str__(self): return an f-string formatted as 'The balance is $X,XXX.XX' using the :,.2f format spec on self.__balance.

content.body: The double underscore prefix (__balance) makes the attribute private and it cannot be accessed directly from outside the class. This is encapsulation: the class controls how its data is read and modified. The __str__ method is Python's convention for defining what an object looks like when printed. When you write print(savings), Python calls savings.__str__() automatically.

rows: Attribute is still technically accessible from outside the class
Use double underscore __balance for true name mangling
Every method must have self as its first parameter
Writing balance += amount instead of self.__balance += amount
Local variable: balance disappears when method ends
Always use self.__ to reference instance attributes

content.body:
Program 10-10 — Testing the BankAccount Class
This program imports bankaccount2 and creates a BankAccount object. It demonstrates how to call methods and how print(object) triggers __str__ automatically.

items: Import bankaccount2.
Write a main() function. Get a starting balance from the user with float(input(...)).
Create an object: savings = bankaccount2.BankAccount(start_bal).
Get a deposit amount and call savings.deposit(pay).
Print the object directly with print(savings), which calls __str__ automatically.
Get a withdrawal amount and call savings.withdraw(cash).
Print the object again to show the updated balance.
Add the if __name__ == '__main__': guard and call main().

content.body: Notice that print(savings) produces a formatted output without calling savings.get_balance() explicitly. The __str__ method handles the formatting. This is the benefit of defining __str__. Any code that prints your object gets clean output automatically.

Program 10-12 — CellPhone Class with Getters and Setters
Define a CellPhone class with three private attributes and a full set of getter and setter methods. This file contains only the class definition.

items: Define class CellPhone:.
Write __init__(self, manufact, model, price): store the three parameters in private attributes self.__manufact, self.__model, and self.__retail_price.
Write setter methods: set_manufact(self, manufact), set_model(self, model), and set_retail_price(self, price). Each one assigns its parameter to the corresponding private attribute.
Write getter methods: get_manufact(self), get_model(self), and get_retail_price(self). Each one returns its corresponding private attribute.

content.body: Getters and setters are the standard pattern for controlled attribute access. The class decides what validation (if any) happens when you set a value. In this program they are simple pass-throughs, but in a real system set_retail_price() might reject negative values. The class can enforce that rule in one place instead of everywhere the price is used.

Program 10-13 — Testing the CellPhone Class
This program imports cellphone, creates a CellPhone object, and reads its attributes back using getter methods.

items: Import cellphone.
Write a main() function. Get manufacturer, model, and retail price from the user.
Create an object: phone = cellphone.CellPhone(man, mod, retail).
Display the stored data by calling the three getter methods: phone.get_manufact(), phone.get_model(), and phone.get_retail_price(). Format the price with $ and two decimal places.
Add the if __name__ == '__main__': guard and call main().
Test it: try accessing phone.__retail_price directly and observe the AttributeError. This is encapsulation working as intended.

content.body: The last test step is the point. When you try phone.__retail_price directly, Python raises an AttributeError. The double-underscore prefix triggers name mangling. Python renames the attribute internally to _CellPhone__retail_price so it cannot be accessed from outside the class with its original name. This is the enforcement mechanism behind encapsulation.

beforeYouSubmit: bookex-ch10.py contains all four programs in order
Programs 10-9 and 10-12 are class definitions only, with no main() inside them
Programs 10-10 and 10-13 import their class module and include a main() function with the __name__ guard
All four programs run without errors
File has a top-of-file documentation header: Name, abc123, Section, Description
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapter 10 techniques: class definition, __init__ constructor, private attributes with double underscore, getter and setter methods, __str__ special method, object instantiation, and dot notation method calls.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read the step-by-step breakdown in your Chapter 10 BookEx Reference Guide. Class syntax errors are often indentation issues
If you get AttributeError on a method call, check that self is the first parameter in every method definition
If your class program file has a main() in it, remove it. Programs 10-9 and 10-12 are class-only files
Submit to CodeGrade early and read the test output carefully
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 10 BookEx Reference Guide, which includes annotated code for all four programs with explanations of __init__, private attributes, and the __str__ method
